html:
XXX

Approach the chamber, look upon his bed.
His is the passing of no peaceful ghost,
Which, as the lark arises to the sky,
Mid morningтАЩs sweetest breeze and softest dew,
Is wingтАЩd to heaven by good menтАЩs sighs and tears!тБатАФ
Anselm parts otherwise.

Old Play

During the interval of quiet which followed the first success of the besiegers, while the one party was preparing to pursue their advantage, and the other to strengthen their means of defence, the Templar and De Bracy held brief council together in the hall of the castle.

тАЬWhere is Front-de-Boeuf?тАЭ said the latter, who had superintended the defence of the fortress on the other side; тАЬmen say he hath been slain.тАЭ

тАЬHe lives,тАЭ said the Templar, coolly, тАЬlives as yet; but had he worn the bullтАЩs head of which he bears the name, and ten plates of iron to fence it withal, he must have gone down before yonder fatal axe. Yet a few hours, and Front-de-Boeuf is with his fathersтБатАФa powerful limb lopped off Prince JohnтАЩs enterprise.тАЭ

тАЬAnd a brave addition to the kingdom of Satan,тАЭ said De Bracy; тАЬthis comes of reviling saints and angels, and ordering images of holy things and holy men to be flung down on the heads of these rascaille yeomen.тАЭ

тАЬGo toтБатАФthou art a fool,тАЭ said the Templar; тАЬthy superstition is upon a level with Front-de-BoeufтАЩs want of faith; neither of you can render a reason for your belief or unbelief.тАЭ

тАЬBenedicite, Sir Templar,тАЭ replied De Bracy, тАЬpray you to keep better rule with your tongue when I am the theme of it. By the Mother of Heaven, I am a better Christian man than thou and thy fellowship; for the bruit goeth shrewdly out, that the most holy Order of the Temple of Zion nurseth not a few heretics within its bosom, and that Sir Brian de Bois-Guilbert is of the number.тАЭ

тАЬCare not thou for such reports,тАЭ said the Templar; тАЬbut let us think of making good the castle.тБатАФHow fought these villain yeomen on thy side?тАЭ

тАЬLike fiends incarnate,тАЭ said De Bracy. тАЬThey swarmed close up to the walls, headed, as I think, by the knave who won the prize at the archery, for I knew his horn and baldric. And this is old FitzurseтАЩs boasted policy, encouraging these malapert knaves to rebel against us! Had I not been armed in proof, the villain had marked me down seven times with as little remorse as if I had been a buck in season. He told every rivet on my armour with a cloth-yard shaft, that rapped against my ribs with as little compunction as if my bones had been of ironтБатАФBut that I wore a shirt of Spanish mail under my plate-coat, I had been fairly sped.тАЭ

тАЬBut you maintained your post?тАЭ said the Templar. тАЬWe lost the outwork on our part.тАЭ

тАЬThat is a shrewd loss,тАЭ said De Bracy; тАЬthe knaves will find cover there to assault the castle more closely, and may, if not well watched, gain some unguarded corner of a tower, or some forgotten window, and so break in upon us. Our numbers are too few for the defence of every point, and the men complain that they can nowhere show themselves, but they are the mark for as many arrows as a parish-butt on a holyday even. Front-de-Boeuf is dying too, so we shall receive no more aid from his bullтАЩs head and brutal strength. How think you, Sir Brian, were we not better make a virtue of necessity, and compound with the rogues by delivering up our prisoners?тАЭ

тАЬHow?тАЭ exclaimed the Templar; тАЬdeliver up our prisoners, and stand an object alike of ridicule and execration, as the doughty warriors who dared by a night-attack to possess themselves of the persons of a party of defenceless travellers, yet could not make good a strong castle against a vagabond troop of outlaws, led by swineherds, jesters, and the very refuse of mankind?тБатАФShame on thy counsel, Maurice de Bracy!тБатАФThe ruins of this castle shall bury both my body and my shame, ere I consent to such base and dishonourable composition.тАЭ

тАЬLet us to the walls, then,тАЭ said De Bracy, carelessly; тАЬthat man never breathed, be he Turk or Templar, who held life at lighter rate than I do. But I trust there is no dishonour in wishing I had here some two scores of my gallant troop of Free Companions?тБатАФOh, my brave lances! if ye knew but how hard your captain were this day bested, how soon should I see my banner at the head of your clump of spears! And how short while would these rabble villains stand to endure your encounter!тАЭ

тАЬWish for whom thou wilt,тАЭ said the Templar, тАЬbut let us make what defence we can with the soldiers who remainтБатАФThey are chiefly Front-de-BoeufтАЩs followers, hated by the English for a thousand acts of insolence and oppression.тАЭ

тАЬThe better,тАЭ said De Bracy; тАЬthe rugged slaves will defend themselves to the last drop of their blood, ere they encounter the revenge of the peasants without. Let us up and be doing, then, Brian de Bois-Guilbert; and, live or die, thou shalt see Maurice de Bracy bear himself this day as a gentleman of blood and lineage.тАЭ

тАЬTo the walls!тАЭ answered the Templar; and they both ascended the battlements to do all that skill could dictate, and manhood accomplish, in defence of the place. They readily agreed that the point of greatest danger was that opposite to the outwork of which the assailants had possessed themselves. The castle, indeed, was divided from that barbican by the moat, and it was impossible that the besiegers could assail the postern-door, with which the outwork corresponded, without surmounting that obstacle; but it was the opinion both of the Templar and De Bracy, that the besiegers, if governed by the same policy their leader had already displayed, would endeavour, by a formidable assault, to draw the chief part of the defendersтАЩ observation to this point, and take measures to avail themselves of every negligence which might take place in the defence elsewhere. To guard against such an evil, their numbers only permitted the knights to place sentinels from space to space along the walls in communication with each other, who might give the alarm whenever danger was threatened. Meanwhile, they agreed that De Bracy should command the defence at the postern, and the Templar should keep with him a score of men or thereabouts as a body of reserve, ready to hasten to any other point which might be suddenly threatened. The loss of the barbican had also this unfortunate effect, that, notwithstanding the superior height of the castle walls, the besieged could not see from them, with the same precision as before, the operations of the enemy; for some straggling underwood approached so near the sallyport of the outwork, that the assailants might introduce into it whatever force they thought proper, not only under cover, but even without the knowledge of the defenders. Utterly uncertain, therefore, upon what point the storm was to burst, De Bracy and his companion were under the necessity of providing against every possible contingency, and their followers, however brave, experienced the anxious dejection of mind incident to men enclosed by enemies, who possessed the power of choosing their time and mode of attack.

Meanwhile, the lord of the beleaguered and endangered castle lay upon a bed of bodily pain and mental agony. He had not the usual resource of bigots in that superstitious period, most of whom were wont to atone for the crimes they were guilty of by liberality to the church, stupefying by this means their terrors by the idea of atonement and forgiveness; and although the refuge which success thus purchased, was no more like to the peace of mind which follows on sincere repentance, than the turbid stupefaction procured by opium resembles healthy and natural slumbers, it was still a state of mind preferable to the agonies of awakened remorse. But among the vices of Front-de-Boeuf, a hard and griping man, avarice was predominant; and he preferred setting church and churchmen at defiance, to purchasing from them pardon and absolution at the price of treasure and of manors. Nor did the Templar, an infidel of another stamp, justly characterise his associate, when he said Front-de-Boeuf could assign no cause for his unbelief and contempt for the established faith; for the Baron would have alleged that the Church sold her wares too dear, that the spiritual freedom which she put up to sale was only to be bought like that of the chief captain of Jerusalem, тАЬwith a great sum,тАЭ and Front-de-Boeuf preferred denying the virtue of the medicine, to paying the expense of the physician.

But the moment had now arrived when earth and all his treasures were gliding from before his eyes, and when the savage BaronтАЩs heart, though hard as a nether millstone, became appalled as he gazed forward into the waste darkness of futurity. The fever of his body aided the impatience and agony of his mind, and his deathbed exhibited a mixture of the newly awakened feelings of horror, combating with the fixed and inveterate obstinacy of his disposition;тБатАФa fearful state of mind, only to be equalled in those tremendous regions, where there are complaints without hope, remorse without repentance, a dreadful sense of present agony, and a presentiment that it cannot cease or be diminished!

тАЬWhere be these dog-priests now,тАЭ growled the Baron, тАЬwho set such price on their ghostly mummery?тБатАФwhere be all those unshod Carmelites, for whom old Front-de-Boeuf founded the convent of St.┬аAnne, robbing his heir of many a fair rood of meadow, and many a fat field and closeтБатАФwhere be the greedy hounds now?тБатАФSwilling, I warrant me, at the ale, or playing their juggling tricks at the bedside of some miserly churl.тБатАФMe, the heir of their founderтБатАФme, whom their foundation binds them to pray forтБатАФmeтБатАФungrateful villains as they are!тБатАФthey suffer to die like the houseless dog on yonder common, unshriven and unhouseled!тБатАФTell the Templar to come hitherтБатАФhe is a priest, and may do somethingтБатАФBut no!тБатАФas well confess myself to the devil as to Brian de Bois-Guilbert, who recks neither of heaven nor of hell.тБатАФI have heard old men talk of prayerтБатАФprayer by their own voiceтБатАФSuch need not to court or to bribe the false priestтБатАФBut IтБатАФI dare not!тАЭ

тАЬLives Reginald Front-de-Boeuf,тАЭ said a broken and shrill voice close by his bedside, тАЬto say there is that which he dares not!тАЭ

The evil conscience and the shaken nerves of Front-de-Boeuf heard, in this strange interruption to his soliloquy, the voice of one of those demons, who, as the superstition of the times believed, beset the beds of dying men to distract their thoughts, and turn them from the meditations which concerned their eternal welfare. He shuddered and drew himself together; but, instantly summoning up his wonted resolution, he exclaimed, тАЬWho is there?тБатАФwhat art thou, that darest to echo my words in a tone like that of the night-raven?тБатАФCome before my couch that I may see thee.тАЭ

тАЬI am thine evil angel, Reginald Front-de-Boeuf,тАЭ replied the voice.

тАЬLet me behold thee then in thy bodily shape, if thou beтАЩst indeed a fiend,тАЭ replied the dying knight; тАЬthink not that I will blench from thee.тБатАФBy the eternal dungeon, could I but grapple with these horrors that hover round me, as I have done with mortal dangers, heaven or hell should never say that I shrunk from the conflict!тАЭ

тАЬThink on thy sins, Reginald Front-de-Boeuf,тАЭ said the almost unearthly voice, тАЬon rebellion, on rapine, on murder!тБатАФWho stirred up the licentious John to war against his grey-headed fatherтБатАФagainst his generous brother?тАЭ

тАЬBe thou fiend, priest, or devil,тАЭ replied Front-de-Boeuf, тАЬthou liest in thy throat!тБатАФNot I stirred John to rebellionтБатАФnot I aloneтБатАФthere were fifty knights and barons, the flower of the midland countiesтБатАФbetter men never laid lance in restтБатАФAnd must I answer for the fault done by fifty?тБатАФFalse fiend, I defy thee! Depart, and haunt my couch no moreтБатАФlet me die in peace if thou be mortalтБатАФif thou be a demon, thy time is not yet come.тАЭ

тАЬIn peace thou shalt not die,тАЭ repeated the voice; тАЬeven in death shalt thou think on thy murdersтБатАФon the groans which this castle has echoedтБатАФon the blood that is engrained in its floors!тАЭ

тАЬThou canst not shake me by thy petty malice,тАЭ answered Front-de-Boeuf, with a ghastly and constrained laugh. тАЬThe infidel JewтБатАФit was merit with heaven to deal with him as I did, else wherefore are men canonized who dip their hands in the blood of Saracens?тБатАФThe Saxon porkers, whom I have slain, they were the foes of my country, and of my lineage, and of my liege lord.тБатАФHo! ho! thou seest there is no crevice in my coat of plateтБатАФArt thou fled?тБатАФart thou silenced?тАЭ

тАЬNo, foul parricide!тАЭ replied the voice; тАЬthink of thy father!тБатАФthink of his death!тБатАФthink of his banquet-room flooded with his gore, and that poured forth by the hand of a son!тАЭ

тАЬHa!тАЭ answered the Baron, after a long pause, тАЬan thou knowest that, thou art indeed the author of evil, and as omniscient as the monks call thee!тБатАФThat secret I deemed locked in my own breast, and in that of one besidesтБатАФthe temptress, the partaker of my guilt.тБатАФGo, leave me, fiend! and seek the Saxon witch Ulrica, who alone could tell thee what she and I alone witnessed.тБатАФGo, I say, to her, who washed the wounds, and straighted the corpse, and gave to the slain man the outward show of one parted in time and in the course of natureтБатАФGo to her, she was my temptress, the foul provoker, the more foul rewarder, of the deedтБатАФlet her, as well as I, taste of the tortures which anticipate hell!тАЭ

тАЬShe already tastes them,тАЭ said Ulrica, stepping before the couch of Front-de-Boeuf; тАЬshe hath long drunken of this cup, and its bitterness is now sweetened to see that thou dost partake it.тБатАФGrind not thy teeth, Front-de-BoeufтБатАФroll not thine eyesтБатАФclench not thine hand, nor shake it at me with that gesture of menace!тБатАФThe hand which, like that of thy renowned ancestor who gained thy name, could have broken with one stroke the skull of a mountain-bull, is now unnerved and powerless as mine own!тАЭ

тАЬVile murderous hag!тАЭ replied Front-de-Boeuf; тАЬdetestable screech-owl! it is then thou who art come to exult over the ruins thou hast assisted to lay low?тАЭ

тАЬAy, Reginald Front-de-Boeuf,тАЭ answered she, тАЬit is Ulrica!тБатАФit is the daughter of the murdered Torquil Wolfganger!тБатАФit is the sister of his slaughtered sons!тБатАФit is she who demands of thee, and of thy fatherтАЩs house, father and kindred, name and fameтБатАФall that she has lost by the name of Front-de-Boeuf!тБатАФThink of my wrongs, Front-de-Boeuf, and answer me if I speak not truth. Thou hast been my evil angel, and I will be thineтБатАФI will dog thee till the very instant of dissolution!тАЭ

тАЬDetestable fury!тАЭ exclaimed Front-de-Boeuf, тАЬthat moment shalt thou never witnessтБатАФHo! Giles, Clement, and Eustace! Saint Maur, and Stephen! seize this damned witch, and hurl her from the battlements headlongтБатАФshe has betrayed us to the Saxon!тБатАФHo! Saint Maur! Clement! false-hearted, knaves, where tarry ye?тАЭ

тАЬCall on them again, valiant Baron,тАЭ said the hag, with a smile of grisly mockery; тАЬsummon thy vassals around thee, doom them that loiter to the scourge and the dungeonтБатАФBut know, mighty chief,тАЭ she continued, suddenly changing her tone, тАЬthou shalt have neither answer, nor aid, nor obedience at their hands.тБатАФListen to these horrid sounds,тАЭ for the din of the recommenced assault and defence now rung fearfully loud from the battlements of the castle; тАЬin that war-cry is the downfall of thy houseтБатАФThe blood-cemented fabric of Front-de-BoeufтАЩs power totters to the foundation, and before the foes he most despised!тБатАФThe Saxon, Reginald!тБатАФthe scorned Saxon assails thy walls!тБатАФWhy liest thou here, like a worn-out hind, when the Saxon storms thy place of strength?тАЭ

тАЬGods and fiends!тАЭ exclaimed the wounded knight; тАЬO, for one momentтАЩs strength, to drag myself to the melee, and perish as becomes my name!тАЭ

тАЬThink not of it, valiant warrior!тАЭ replied she; тАЬthou shalt die no soldierтАЩs death, but perish like the fox in his den, when the peasants have set fire to the cover around it.тАЭ

тАЬHateful hag! thou liest!тАЭ exclaimed Front-de-Boeuf; тАЬmy followers bear them bravelyтБатАФmy walls are strong and highтБатАФmy comrades in arms fear not a whole host of Saxons, were they headed by Hengist and Horsa!тБатАФThe war-cry of the Templar and of the Free Companions rises high over the conflict! And by mine honour, when we kindle the blazing beacon, for joy of our defence, it shall consume thee, body and bones; and I shall live to hear thou art gone from earthly fires to those of that hell, which never sent forth an incarnate fiend more utterly diabolical!тАЭ

тАЬHold thy belief,тАЭ replied Ulrica, тАЬtill the proof reach theeтБатАФBut, no!тАЭ she said, interrupting herself, тАЬthou shalt know, even now, the doom, which all thy power, strength, and courage, is unable to avoid, though it is prepared for thee by this feeble band. Markest thou the smouldering and suffocating vapour which already eddies in sable folds through the chamber?тБатАФDidst thou think it was but the darkening of thy bursting eyesтБатАФthe difficulty of thy cumbered breathing?тБатАФNo! Front-de-Boeuf, there is another causeтБатАФRememberest thou the magazine of fuel that is stored beneath these apartments?тАЭ

тАЬWoman!тАЭ he exclaimed with fury, тАЬthou hast not set fire to it?тБатАФBy heaven, thou hast, and the castle is in flames!тАЭ

тАЬThey are fast rising at least,тАЭ said Ulrica, with frightful composure; тАЬand a signal shall soon wave to warn the besiegers to press hard upon those who would extinguish them.тБатАФFarewell, Front-de-Boeuf!тБатАФMay Mista, Skogula, and Zernebock, gods of the ancient SaxonsтБатАФfiends, as the priests now call themтБатАФsupply the place of comforters at your dying bed, which Ulrica now relinquishes!тБатАФBut know, if it will give thee comfort to know it, that Ulrica is bound to the same dark coast with thyself, the companion of thy punishment as the companion of thy guilt.тБатАФAnd now, parricide, farewell forever!тБатАФMay each stone of this vaulted roof find a tongue to echo that title into thine ear!тАЭ

So saying, she left the apartment; and Front-de-Boeuf could hear the crash of the ponderous key, as she locked and double-locked the door behind her, thus cutting off the most slender chance of escape. In the extremity of agony he shouted upon his servants and alliesтБатАФтАЬStephen and Saint Maur!тБатАФClement and Giles!тБатАФI burn here unaided!тБатАФTo the rescueтБатАФto the rescue, brave Bois-Guilbert, valiant De Bracy!тБатАФIt is Front-de-Boeuf who calls!тБатАФIt is your master, ye traitor squires!тБатАФYour allyтБатАФyour brother in arms, ye perjured and faithless knights!тБатАФall the curses due to traitors upon your recreant heads, do you abandon me to perish thus miserably!тБатАФThey hear me notтБатАФthey cannot hear meтБатАФmy voice is lost in the din of battle.тБатАФThe smoke rolls thicker and thickerтБатАФthe fire has caught upon the floor belowтБатАФO, for one drought of the air of heaven, were it to be purchased by instant annihilation!тАЭ And in the mad frenzy of despair, the wretch now shouted with the shouts of the fighters, now muttered curses on himself, on mankind, and on Heaven itself.тБатАФтАЬThe red fire flashes through the thick smoke!тАЭ he exclaimed; тАЬthe demon marches against me under the banner of his own elementтБатАФFoul spirit, avoid!тБатАФI go not with thee without my comradesтБатАФall, all are thine, that garrison these wallsтБатАФThinkest thou Front-de-Boeuf will be singled out to go alone?тБатАФNoтБатАФthe infidel TemplarтБатАФthe licentious De BracyтБатАФUlrica, the foul murdering strumpetтБатАФthe men who aided my enterprisesтБатАФthe dog Saxons and accursed Jews, who are my prisonersтБатАФall, all shall attend meтБатАФa goodly fellowship as ever took the downward roadтБатАФHa, ha, ha!тАЭ and he laughed in his frenzy till the vaulted roof rang again. тАЬWho laughed there?тАЭ exclaimed Front-de-Boeuf, in altered mood, for the noise of the conflict did not prevent the echoes of his own mad laughter from returning upon his earтБатАФтАЬwho laughed there?тБатАФUlrica, was it thou?тБатАФSpeak, witch, and I forgive theeтБатАФfor, only thou or the fiend of hell himself could have laughed at such a moment. AvauntтБатАФavaunt!тБатАФтАЭ

But it were impious to trace any farther the picture of the blasphemer and parricideтАЩs deathbed.
